quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Standard deviation (s)"?

options.text: One-sample t-test applied to the differences: t = d̄/(sd/√n) with df = n-1, testing H₀: μd = 0
The square root of variance; measures the typical distance of observations from the mean in the original units of measurement
The proportion of variation in y explained by the linear relationship with x; ranges from 0 to 1
The mean of a random variable: μ = Σ[x·P(x)]; represents the long-run average outcome over many repetitions

quiz_questions.explanation: "Standard deviation (s)" is defined as: The square root of variance; measures the typical distance of observations from the mean in the original units of measurement. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Variance (s²)"?

options.text: Tests H₀: p₁ = p₂ using pooled proportion p̂c and z = (p̂₁ - p̂₂)/SE_pooled
Tests H₀: μ = μ₀ using t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) with df = n - 1; used when σ is unknown
The average of the squared deviations from the mean, calculated using n-1 in the denominator for sample data (Bessel's correction)
Randomly selecting entire groups (clusters) and surveying all individuals within chosen clusters; practical when a population list is unavailable

quiz_questions.explanation: "Variance (s²)" is defined as: The average of the squared deviations from the mean, calculated using n-1 in the denominator for sample data (Bessel's correction). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Range"?

options.text: A variable that takes a finite or countably infinite number of specific values, each with a definite probability
The claim we suspect is true and are trying to find evidence for; can be one-sided () or two-sided (≠)
The variable suspected of influencing or predicting the response variable in a study
The difference between the maximum and minimum values; simple but highly sensitive to outliers

quiz_questions.explanation: "Range" is defined as: The difference between the maximum and minimum values; simple but highly sensitive to outliers. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Degrees of freedom"?

options.text: For Y = a + bX: E(Y) = a + b·E(X) and Var(Y) = b²·Var(X); adding a constant shifts the mean but does not change spread
In the sample variance formula, n-1 degrees of freedom accounts for the fact that the sample mean is estimated from the data
Using df = min(n₁-1, n₂-1) when technology is unavailable; produces wider intervals and larger p-values
Taking the logarithm of variable values to linearize exponential or power relationships for regression analysis

quiz_questions.explanation: "Degrees of freedom" is defined as: In the sample variance formula, n-1 degrees of freedom accounts for the fact that the sample mean is estimated from the data. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Coefficient of variation"?

options.text: A variable not included in the study that may influence the relationship between the variables being studied
The distribution of counts for every combination of categories in a two-way table, expressed as proportions of the grand total
Standard deviation divided by the mean, expressed as a percentage; useful for comparing variability across datasets with different units or scales
σp̂ = √(p(1-p)/n); decreases as n increases, meaning larger samples give more precise estimates

quiz_questions.explanation: "Coefficient of variation" is defined as: Standard deviation divided by the mean, expressed as a percentage; useful for comparing variability across datasets with different units or scales. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The square root of variance; measures the typical distance of observations from the mean in the original units of measurement"?

options.text: Standard normal distribution
Power
Standard deviation (s)
t-test for slope

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Standard deviation (s)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The average of the squared deviations from the mean, calculated using n-1 in the denominator for sample data (Bessel's correction)"?

options.text: Variance (s²)
Retrospective vs. prospective study
Heavy tails
Response bias

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Variance (s²)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The difference between the maximum and minimum values; simple but highly sensitive to outliers"?

options.text: Z-score
Random assignment
Range
Null hypothesis for slope

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Range".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "In the sample variance formula, n-1 degrees of freedom accounts for the fact that the sample mean is estimated from the data"?

options.text: Heavy tails
Significance test for μ
Explanatory variable
Degrees of freedom

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Degrees of freedom".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Standard deviation divided by the mean, expressed as a percentage; useful for comparing variability across datasets with different units or scales"?

options.text: Mean difference (d̄)
Significance level (α)
Type II error
Coefficient of variation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Coefficient of variation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Measures of Variability is accurate?

options.text: Probability quantifies uncertainty on a scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), with the Law of Large Numbers stating that relative frequency approaches true probability as the number of trials increases.
Measures of Variability quantify the spread or dispersion of data, describing how much individual observations deviate from the center.
The interval provides a range of plausible values for the rate of change in y per unit change in x.
Interpreting Chi-Square Results requires understanding what a significant χ² value means and what it does not.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Measures of Variability as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Measures of Variability?

options.text: Two-way tables (contingency tables) organize counts for two categorical variables and allow calculation of marginal, joint, and conditional distributions.
For independent random variables: Var(X ± Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y)—variances always add, even for differences.
Standard deviation (s) measures the typical distance of observations from the mean, while variance (s²) is its squared counterpart used in many formulas.
Common transformations include log(y) vs. x for exponential relationships and log(y) vs. log(x) for power relationships, with back-transformation needed to interpret predictions in original units.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Measures of Variability as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Measures of Variability?

options.text: Hypotheses and P-Values form the logical framework for statistical significance testing.
Confidence Intervals for Proportions estimate an unknown population proportion p using the sample proportion p̂ ± z*√(p̂(1-p̂)/n), where z* is the critical value for the desired confidence level.
One-sided tests (Hₐ: p > p₀ or p < p₀) and two-sided tests (Hₐ: p ≠ p₀) require different p-value calculations.
Range, IQR, and standard deviation each capture different aspects of spread, and choosing the right measure depends on the data's shape and the presence of outliers.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Measures of Variability as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Measures of Variability?

options.text: Probability quantifies uncertainty on a scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), with the Law of Large Numbers stating that relative frequency approaches true probability as the number of trials increases.
Residual plots—graphs of residuals versus x—are essential diagnostic tools: a random scatter indicates the linear model is appropriate, while patterns suggest non-linearity.
The choice of transformation depends on the curvature pattern, and predictions must be back-transformed to the original scale.
Measures of Variability quantify the spread or dispersion of data, describing how much individual observations deviate from the center.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Measures of Variability as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Measures of Variability?

options.text: Blocking groups experimental units by a known source of variability (such as age, gender, or location) before random assignment within each block, increasing the experiment's ability to detect treatment effects.
The interval provides a range of plausible values for the rate of change in y per unit change in x.
When a residual plot from a linear fit shows a clear curve, transforming x, y, or both can create a linear relationship suitable for least-squares regression.
Standard deviation (s) measures the typical distance of observations from the mean, while variance (s²) is its squared counterpart used in many formulas.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Measures of Variability as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Measures of Variability?

options.text: Comparing investment portfolio risk by measuring standard deviation of annual returns—higher σ indicates greater volatility and risk
Pharmaceutical trials determining sample size needed so the sampling distribution of the cure rate is narrow enough to detect a clinically meaningful effect
Using boxplots and five-number summaries to compare test score distributions across multiple schools or demographic groups
Manufacturing process monitoring using the sampling distribution of x̄ to set control chart limits for detecting when a process mean shifts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Measures of Variability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Measures of Variability?

options.text: Sociological research examining whether political party affiliation is independent of geographic region using survey data from a national random sample
Manufacturing tolerance analysis using variance to ensure parts fall within acceptable dimensional limits
News organizations reporting poll results with margin of error ('52% favor the measure ± 3 percentage points') derived from the confidence interval formula
Election polling using CLT to justify normal-based margin of error calculations even though individual voter responses (yes/no) are binary, not normal

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Measures of Variability.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Measures of Variability applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Educational researchers using a one-sample t-test to determine whether average ACT scores in a district differ from the national mean of 21
Manufacturing process monitoring using the sampling distribution of x̄ to set control chart limits for detecting when a process mean shifts
Analyzing the relationship between study hours and exam scores to determine what proportion (r²) of score variation is explained by study time
Evaluating consistency in athletic performance (e.g., comparing two pitchers' speed variability using standard deviation)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Measures of Variability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Measures of Variability?

options.text: Comparing investment portfolio risk by measuring standard deviation of annual returns—higher σ indicates greater volatility and risk
Laboratory experiments controlling temperature, humidity, and light exposure when testing plant growth under different fertilizer treatments
Clinical trials comparing cure rates between drug and placebo groups to determine whether the drug is more effective than no treatment
Tire wear studies using matched-pairs design: placing competing tire brands on opposite sides of the same car to control for driving conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Measures of Variability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Measures of Variability in practice?

options.text: Environmental scientists using residual plots to verify that a linear model is appropriate for predicting water quality from upstream land use data
Manufacturing tolerance analysis using variance to ensure parts fall within acceptable dimensional limits
Testing whether advertising spending significantly predicts sales revenue (H₀: β₁ = 0) to justify continued marketing budget allocation
Online survey nonresponse bias where only highly motivated or opinionated individuals choose to respond, skewing results toward extreme views

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Measures of Variability.
